title: Analysis of Unconfined Flow Induced by Constant Rate Pumping Based on the Lagging Theory

research.question: Can two lag times embedded in Darcy's law represent delayed capillary-fringe drainage during constant-rate pumping and improve unconfined-aquifer parameter estimates?
research.system: A homogeneous anisotropic unconfined aquifer of finite saturated thickness, pumped by a partially penetrating finite-radius well with wellbore storage and observed at radial distances from the well.
research.inputData: Constant-rate pumping-test drawdown records from Cape Cod, Massachusetts; Canadian Forces Base Borden, Ontario; and Saint Pardon de Conques, Gironde, together with site geometry and pumping conditions reported for those tests.
research.method: Introduce flux and drawdown lag times into the free-surface condition, solve the resulting axisymmetric model by Laplace and Weber transforms with numerical inversion, examine normalized sensitivities, and estimate hydraulic and lag parameters by nonlinear least squares.
research.benchmark: Compare predictions and field-data fits against the Neuman, Moench, Tartakovsky-Neuman, and Malama solutions using standard error of estimate and mean error.
research.uncertainty: The two lag times are phenomenological parameters rather than direct unsaturated-zone measurements; fitted values vary with observation distance, and the Borden flux-lag trend is an explicit exception to the otherwise near-linear distance relation.
research.conclusion: The two-lag model fits the three field data sets well, yields specific-yield estimates in laboratory-supported ranges, and attributes stronger drawdown sensitivity to the drawdown lag than to the flux lag.

narrative.throughline: Follow an S-shaped pumping response from its delayed capillary-fringe mechanism, through a two-lag free-surface law, to field-calibrated drawdown curves and distance-dependent lag times.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience knows that constant-rate pumping lowers an unconfined water table but may expect drainage to respond instantaneously.
narrative.stakes: Treating gravity drainage as instantaneous can distort the intermediate-time response and produce anomalously small specific-yield estimates.
narrative.resolution: Two distinct lag times make the delayed release visible, remain reducible to established free-surface models, and improve agreement with pumping-test observations.

background.premise: Measured unconfined-aquifer drawdown commonly forms an S-shaped curve with early, intermediate, and late segments supplied by different storage mechanisms.
background.visibleEvidence: A time-drawdown trace bends through three labeled segments as aquifer storage gives way to gravity drainage and later combined release.
background.whyNow: The segmented curve is the observable phenomenon that the lagging model must explain.
background.boundary: The segmentation describes constant-rate pumping in an unconfined aquifer, not every pumping configuration.
background.sourceExcerpt: continues long enough. The temporal distribution of measured drawdown data
background.visualObject: Three-segment time-drawdown curve beside a pumping well cross-section
background.motionPurpose: Reveal the observed temporal structure before introducing a mechanism
background.stepDetail: Trace the curve from the first segment through the delayed middle segment to the late segment while the contributing storage regions illuminate in sequence.
background.transitionBridge: Hold on the intermediate bend and move upward to the capillary fringe that delays drainage.

background.premise: Capillary force retains pore water above a declining water table, so drainage takes time and requires an explicit time lag.
background.visibleEvidence: Menisci in large and small pores drain at different suction levels while water remains temporarily stored above the falling water table.
background.whyNow: This delayed release supplies the physical rationale for lagging the vertical flux and water-table response.
background.boundary: The model summarizes capillary-fringe behavior phenomenologically rather than resolving a full variably saturated flow field.
background.sourceExcerpt: takes time to complete the drainage, and the time lag
background.visualObject: Falling water table with retained capillary water and pore-scale menisci
background.motionPurpose: Make the delayed drainage mechanism spatially concrete
background.stepDetail: Lower the water table first, keep water suspended in the capillary fringe, and release it progressively toward the aquifer.
background.transitionBridge: Compress the pore-scale delay into two clocks attached to the free surface.

background.premise: Instantaneous complete drainage at the water table can fail to provide an accurate estimate of specific yield.
background.visibleEvidence: An instantaneous-drainage model curve misses the delayed segment and points to a lower specific-yield estimate.
background.whyNow: Parameter bias turns the drainage mechanism into a practical aquifer-test interpretation problem.
background.boundary: The source states that failure may occur; it does not claim that every instantaneous-drainage fit is inaccurate.
background.sourceExcerpt: water table may sometimes fail to give an accurate estimate
background.visualObject: Two competing free-surface responses linked to contrasting specific-yield estimates
background.motionPurpose: Connect a modeling assumption to an estimable hydraulic parameter
background.stepDetail: Overlay instantaneous and delayed drainage responses, then highlight the specific-yield value associated with the mismatched curve.
background.transitionBridge: Replace the instantaneous free-surface arrow with a lag-aware governing system.

methods.input: Axisymmetric drawdown in a homogeneous anisotropic unconfined aquifer
methods.output: A radial-vertical groundwater-flow equation for drawdown
methods.validity: The aquifer is treated as homogeneous and anisotropic with radial symmetry around the pumping well.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Model Description, Equation 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: governing equation describing the drawdown distribution in the unconﬁned aquifer
methods.visualObject: Cylindrical aquifer domain with radial and vertical derivative axes
methods.motionPurpose: Establish the field equation that all later boundary physics constrain
methods.stepDetail: Map radial and vertical conductivity terms onto the aquifer cross-section and expose the drawdown field inside it.
methods.transitionBridge: Move from the aquifer interior to the finite-radius pumping boundary.

methods.input: Pumping discharge at a partially penetrating finite-radius well
methods.output: An inner flux condition containing wellbore storage
methods.validity: The storage term applies at the pumping well and uses the modeled pumping-well water level.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.1 Model Description, Equation 3
methods.sourceExcerpt: term of equation (3) represents the water ﬂux across the
methods.visualObject: Pumping-well cylinder with an inflow arrow and a changing internal water column
methods.motionPurpose: Separate aquifer inflow from water released by the well casing
methods.stepDetail: Route part of the imposed discharge through the aquifer wall and the remainder through the changing well water level.
methods.transitionBridge: Lift the view from the well screen to the moving free surface.

methods.input: Vertical Darcy flux and aquifer drawdown at the free surface
methods.output: A free-surface relation with flux lag tau_q and drawdown lag tau_s
methods.validity: tau_q represents rapid capillary-fringe release linked to vertical hydraulic gradient; tau_s represents delayed excess-storage drainage linked to capillary suction.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.1 Model Description, Equation 7b
methods.sourceExcerpt: lag times [T] of the ﬂux and aquifer drawdown, respectively.
methods.visualObject: Free surface carrying separate flux and drawdown clock markers
methods.motionPurpose: Assign distinct physical roles to the two delay parameters
methods.stepDetail: Offset the vertical-flux arrow by tau_q and the free-surface displacement by tau_s on a common time axis.
methods.transitionBridge: Expand the delayed quantities into a tractable first-order relation.

methods.input: Time-shifted flux and drawdown terms in the lagged free-surface relation
methods.output: A first-order Taylor representation of the delayed relation
methods.validity: Only the first-order Taylor representation is retained.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.1 Model Description, Equation 8
methods.sourceExcerpt: Employing the Taylor series expansion, the ﬁrst‐order representation of equation
methods.visualObject: Shifted time traces collapsing into value and first-derivative terms
methods.motionPurpose: Turn conceptual delays into solvable local terms
methods.stepDetail: Replace each shifted quantity with its present value plus its lag-weighted temporal derivative.
methods.transitionBridge: Insert the first-order lag law into the water-table kinematic condition.

methods.input: First-order lag representation and free-surface mass balance
methods.output: A linearized free-surface equation containing tau_q and tau_s
methods.validity: The boundary reduces to the Neuman form when both lag times vanish and to the cited Moench form under its stated parameter correspondence.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.1 Model Description, Equation 9
methods.sourceExcerpt: free surface equation (i.e., Kz∂s/∂z = − Sy∂s/∂t) if neglecting
methods.visualObject: Water-table boundary equation with two highlighted lag coefficients
methods.motionPurpose: Show where the lag physics enters the aquifer model
methods.stepDetail: Substitute the expanded lag law into the free-surface balance and retain the resulting linear boundary terms.
methods.transitionBridge: Rescale the physical domain and parameters for transform solution.

methods.input: Dimensional drawdown variables geometry conductivities storage terms and lag times
methods.output: A dimensionless pumping model and dimensionless lag parameters
methods.validity: The definitions follow Equation 10 and preserve radial anisotropy and well geometry.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2 Dimensionless Solution in Laplace Domain, Equation 10
methods.sourceExcerpt: Domain The dimensionless variables and parameters are deﬁned as follows:
methods.visualObject: Dimensional symbols passing through a scaling frame into dimensionless symbols
methods.motionPurpose: Consolidate scales before applying integral transforms
methods.stepDetail: Scale drawdown, radius, depth, time, conductivities, storage, and both lag times according to Equation 10.
methods.transitionBridge: Send the dimensionless equations into Laplace-time and Weber-radius domains.

methods.input: Dimensionless radial-vertical equations and boundary conditions
methods.output: Governing equations represented in the Laplace parameter p
methods.validity: Appendix A defines the Laplace transform and maps first time derivatives to multiplication by p after applying the initial conditions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Laplace transform, its inverse transform, and property of the ﬁrst derivative
methods.visualObject: Time-dependent equations folding onto a p-axis
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the temporal transform used in the semianalytical solution
methods.stepDetail: Transform each time derivative into the Laplace parameter p.
methods.transitionBridge: The Laplace-domain equations still retain finite-radius radial structure.

methods.input: Laplace-domain radial equations with a finite-radius well boundary
methods.output: Drawdown and well-level amplitudes in the Laplace-Weber domain
methods.validity: The Weber transform accommodates cylindrical lower boundaries of Dirichlet Neumann or Robin type.
methods.locator: PDF page 13, Appendix A, Equations (A4)-(A6); PDF page 5, Equations (17a)-(17b)
methods.sourceExcerpt: the Weber transform, its inverse transform, and property for the Neumann‐type boundary condition are respectively written as
methods.visualObject: Finite-radius radial profiles folding onto Weber parameter a
methods.motionPurpose: Map cylindrical radius independently of the preceding time transform
methods.stepDetail: Transform the radial equations with the Weber kernel.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved amplitudes are ready for inverse radial transformation.

methods.input: Transformed drawdown solution in Weber space
methods.output: A radial drawdown integral in the Laplace domain
methods.validity: The inverse Weber expression is evaluated over the transform variable as given in Equation 18.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 2.2 Dimensionless Solution in Laplace Domain, Equation 18
methods.sourceExcerpt: the inverse Weber transform, the drawdown solution in Laplace domain
methods.visualObject: Weber spectrum integrating back into concentric radial drawdown rings
methods.motionPurpose: Recover spatial drawdown before recovering physical time
methods.stepDetail: Integrate the transformed amplitudes against the Weber kernel to restore radial position.
methods.transitionBridge: Carry the radial solution into numerical inversion of the remaining Laplace coordinate.

methods.input: Radial drawdown solution in the Laplace domain
methods.output: Transient drawdown in the time domain
methods.validity: The paper uses the Crump algorithm for numerical Laplace inversion and numerical quadrature for the radial integral.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 2.2 Dimensionless Solution in Laplace Domain, Equation 18
methods.sourceExcerpt: the time domain is obtained by numerically inverting equation (18)
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain response resolving into a transient time-drawdown curve
methods.motionPurpose: Return the semianalytical solution to the observable time axis
methods.stepDetail: Apply Crump inversion to each observation location and reveal the resulting temporal response.
methods.transitionBridge: Perturb each parameter around the recovered solution to inspect response sensitivity.

methods.input: Transient solution and each hydraulic or lag parameter P_k
methods.output: Dimensionless sensitivity curves X_k for drawdown
methods.validity: The derivative uses a forward finite difference with Delta P_k set to 10^-3 P_k.
methods.locator: PDF page 7, Section 3.2 Sensitivity Analysis, Equations 25-26
methods.sourceExcerpt: forward ﬁnite difference formula is used to approximate the ﬁrst
methods.visualObject: Parameter perturbation controls feeding a family of normalized sensitivity curves
methods.motionPurpose: Rank which parameters most strongly alter temporal drawdown
methods.stepDetail: Perturb one parameter by 10^-3 of its value, evaluate the drawdown difference, and scale the derivative by that parameter.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the sensitivity-informed model to estimate parameters from field curves.

methods.input: Four observed drawdown series from the Cape Cod constant-rate pumping test
methods.output: Cape Cod hydraulic parameter estimates with SEE and ME for four observation wells
methods.validity: The five solutions are coupled to the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm and Table 1 reports Cape Cod estimates and prediction errors.
methods.locator: PDF pages 7-9, Section 3.3 Pumping Test Data Analysis: Cape Cod Site, Table 1 and Figure 4
methods.sourceExcerpt: Table 1 lists the aquifer parameters estimated by the ﬁve
methods.visualObject: Four Cape Cod observation-well panels with five fitted model curves
methods.motionPurpose: Test whether the lagged model explains the Cape Cod pumping response
methods.stepDetail: Fit the five solutions to the four Cape Cod observation-well series.
methods.transitionBridge: The same five-solution fit is applied independently to the Borden observations.

methods.input: Four observed drawdown series from the Canadian Forces Base Borden constant-rate pumping test
methods.output: Borden hydraulic and lag parameter estimates with SEE and ME for four piezometers
methods.validity: The five solutions used for Cape Cod are fitted to the digitized Borden records; Table 2 and Figure 5 report estimates and fit behavior, including the early WD1A limitation.
methods.locator: PDF pages 9-11, Section 3.4 Pumping Test Data Analysis: Borden Site, Table 2 and Figure 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: The recorded drawdown data are analyzed based on the same solutions as those used for the Cape Cod site.
methods.visualObject: Four Borden piezometer panels with five fitted model curves
methods.motionPurpose: Estimate the Borden aquifer and lag parameters as an independent field operation.
methods.stepDetail: Fit the five solutions to WD1A, WD1B, WD2A, and WD4A.
methods.transitionBridge: A separate two-well fit owns the Saint Pardon evidence.

methods.input: Drawdown series from the 10 m and 30 m Saint Pardon de Conques observation wells
methods.output: Saint Pardon hydraulic and lag parameter estimates with SEE and ME for both wells
methods.validity: The estimates and prediction errors are those reported in Table 3; Figure 6 shows the fit differences at 10 m and 30 m.
methods.locator: PDF pages 11-12, Section 3.5 Pumping Test Data Analysis: Saint Pardon de Conques Site, Table 3 and Figure 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: The estimated parameters are listed in Table 3.
methods.visualObject: Two Saint Pardon observation-well panels with five fitted model curves
methods.motionPurpose: Estimate the Saint Pardon aquifer and lag parameters as its own field operation.
methods.stepDetail: Fit the five solutions independently to the 10 m and 30 m records.
methods.transitionBridge: The Cape Cod, Borden, and Saint Pardon fit outputs now return together to the field aquifers.

symbols.transition: Grow from the water-level decline into the full radial-vertical field

symbols.visualObject: Water-level drop inside the pumping well
symbols.meaning: Pumping-well water level relative to its initial condition

symbols.visualObject: Clock on the vertical flux arrow
symbols.meaning: Lag time associated with vertical flux and rapid pore-water release from the capillary fringe
symbols.transition: Shift the flux response along the time axis

symbols.meaning: Lag time associated with drawdown and excess storage retained by capillary suction
symbols.transition: Shift the free-surface response along the time axis
Rotate from the radial arrow to the vertical coordinate

symbols.visualObject: Drainable-water fraction beside the falling water table
symbols.transition: Resolve from retained capillary water into the fitted parameter

symbols.visualObject: Normalized sensitivity curve for parameter k
symbols.meaning: Normalized sensitivity of drawdown to input parameter P_k
symbols.transition: Emerge from a small parameter perturbation and drawdown difference

return.visualObject: Cape Cod, Borden, and Saint Pardon field drawdown panels with their independently fitted present-solution curves and specific-yield estimates
return.claim: Across the three owned site-fit operations, the present two-lag solution gives very good fits to observed drawdown and estimates specific yield within the cited laboratory-supported ranges.
return.claimBoundary: The return consumes only the Cape Cod, Borden, and Saint Pardon fit outputs; it remains conditional on the homogeneous axisymmetric model, linearized free-surface treatment, selected comparison solutions, digitized records, and site-specific fit limitations.
return.locator: PDF page 13, Section 4 Concluding Remarks, finding 4
return.sourceExcerpt: present solution provides very good ﬁts to ﬁeld observed drawdown
return.minimalText: Delayed drainage fits the field response

visual.primaryObject: Finite-radius partially penetrating pumping well in an unconfined aquifer with a moving water table
visual.accentObject: Capillary fringe carrying separate flux-lag and drawdown-lag clocks
visual.geometryNotes: Use an axisymmetric radial-vertical section with saturated thickness b, well radius r_w, observation radius r, and elevation z. Transform the physical section into dimensionless coordinates, then into Laplace-time and Weber-radius domains before reconstructing the three-segment time-drawdown curves.
